title: IQ Percentile Calculator – Score to Population Rank
tagline: Convert any IQ score to a precise population percentile using normal distribution statistics, and get an intelligence classification with full context.
intro: Enter your IQ score to instantly see where you rank among the general population, from the 1st to the 99th percentile.

error.invalid: Please enter a valid IQ score (typically between 40 and 200).
error.range: IQ score appears outside the typical range (40–200). Results may be extrapolated.

about.heading: About the IQ Percentile Calculator
about.body:
IQ, or Intelligence Quotient, is a standardized score derived from a battery of cognitive tests designed to measure general mental ability. The modern IQ scale is anchored at a mean of 100 with a standard deviation of 15 for most major tests, including the Wechsler series (WAIS, WISC). This means that in any large representative sample, approximately 68% of scores fall between 85 and 115 (one standard deviation either side of the mean), and about 95% fall between 70 and 130 (two standard deviations).

The percentile rank answers a different question than the raw score: instead of asking how many points above or below average you scored, it tells you what percentage of the population you outperformed. A percentile of 84 means you scored higher than 84 out of every 100 people in the reference population. This framing is often more intuitive because it puts the score into direct social context. Raw IQ differences in the middle of the distribution feel more meaningful when expressed as percentiles — the difference between IQ 100 (50th percentile) and IQ 115 (84th percentile) is substantial in population terms, even though it is only 15 points.

The conversion from IQ to percentile uses the standard normal distribution, also called the bell curve or Gaussian distribution. The procedure is to first compute a z-score: subtract the test mean (100 for most standard tests) and divide by the standard deviation (15 for Wechsler tests, 24 for Cattell tests, 16 for Stanford-Binet Form L-M). The z-score is then converted to a cumulative probability using the normal distribution CDF, which gives the fraction of the population with a lower score. Multiplying by 100 gives the percentile.

It is important to understand what IQ measures and what it does not. IQ tests assess specific cognitive abilities — primarily fluid reasoning, working memory, processing speed, verbal comprehension, and visual-spatial ability. They do not measure creativity, emotional intelligence, wisdom, practical skills, or social aptitude. High IQ correlates with academic achievement, complex job performance, and certain health outcomes, but it is neither necessary nor sufficient for success in most domains of life. Conversely, average or below-average IQ does not preclude achievement, fulfillment, or social contribution.

IQ scores also have measurement error. Most professionally administered IQ tests have a standard error of measurement (SEM) of around 3–5 points, meaning a reported score of 120 reflects a true score likely somewhere between 115 and 125. For this reason, clinical psychologists typically interpret IQ in ranges and confidence intervals rather than treating a single number as definitive. The percentile ranges shown in this calculator are based on the normative means and standard deviations for the selected test type.

examples.heading: IQ Percentile Examples
examples.intro: Four representative IQ scores showing corresponding z-scores, percentiles, and classifications on a standard scale (mean 100, SD 15).

items.note: z-score = 0. Exactly average — half the population scores higher, half lower. Falls in the Average classification range (90–109).
z-score = +1.0. Scores higher than 84% of the population. High Average classification. One standard deviation above the mean.
z-score = +2.0. Scores higher than approximately 98% of the population. Very Superior classification. Threshold commonly used for gifted programs.
z-score = −1.0. Scores higher than approximately 16% of the population. Low Average classification. One standard deviation below the mean.

howto.heading: How to Use the IQ Percentile Calculator
steps: Enter your IQ score as reported on your official test result — use the full-scale IQ or composite score, not a sub-score.
Select your IQ test type from the dropdown: Standard (Wechsler, mean 100 SD 15), Cattell (mean 100 SD 24), or Stanford-Binet (mean 100 SD 16).
Select your age group if you want the classification label to reflect age-relevant norms (optional — the percentile calculation itself uses the same formula regardless of age).
Click Calculate Percentile to see your population rank, z-score, and intelligence classification.
Use the result as context — discuss the score with a licensed psychologist for clinical interpretation, especially for educational or vocational planning.

items.q: What does the IQ percentile mean?
items.a: The percentile rank tells you what percentage of the population scored below you on the same test. An IQ percentile of 84 means you outperformed 84% of the reference population. It is calculated by converting your IQ score to a z-score and then finding the cumulative probability under the standard normal curve. Unlike the raw IQ point, the percentile immediately communicates your relative position in the population.

items.q: What is the difference between the Wechsler, Cattell, and Stanford-Binet scales?
items.a: All three scales anchor the population average at 100, but they use different standard deviations. The Wechsler tests (WAIS, WISC) and most modern IQ tests use a standard deviation of 15. The Cattell Culture Fair test uses a wider SD of 24, so the same percentile corresponds to a more extreme raw score. The Stanford-Binet Form L-M uses an SD of 16. Selecting the correct scale is important: an IQ of 130 is the 98th percentile on a Wechsler scale but only the 89th percentile on the Cattell scale.

items.q: Is a high IQ sufficient for success in life?
items.a: Research shows that IQ correlates meaningfully with academic achievement, complex job performance, and certain long-term outcomes, but it is neither necessary nor sufficient for success in most areas. Factors such as conscientiousness, emotional regulation, motivation, social skills, and access to resources are equally or more important for practical achievement. Very high-IQ individuals succeed and struggle at similar rates to the general population when these other factors are not controlled.

IQ tends to be relatively stable across adulthood, but it is not fixed. Fluid intelligence — the ability to reason and solve new problems — typically peaks in the mid-20s and gradually declines with age, while crystallized intelligence (accumulated knowledge and vocabulary) often continues to grow into late middle age. Environmental factors including education, nutrition, sleep quality, and physical health can influence IQ scores, especially during childhood and adolescence.

items.q: How accurate is the IQ percentile calculation?
items.a: The percentile calculation in this calculator is mathematically precise given the input score and the assumed normal distribution with the selected mean and SD. However, the calculation assumes the score comes from a test that is properly standardized and that you were tested under valid conditions. IQ tests also have measurement error of approximately 3–5 points, meaning a single test score should be interpreted as a range rather than an exact value.

items.q: What IQ score is considered gifted?
items.a: Definitions of giftedness vary by organization and country, but a common threshold is an IQ of 130 or above on a standard scale (SD 15), which corresponds to approximately the 98th percentile — the top 2% of the population. Some gifted programs use 125 (95th percentile) as a cut-off, and some assessments for highly gifted programs require 145 or above. It is worth noting that many gifted education professionals also consider creativity, motivation, and domain-specific talent alongside IQ.
